subtopic_detail: Atonic PPH second-line uterotonic with TXA
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar at a tertiary maternity unit reviewing a G3P3 woman who had a spontaneous vaginal delivery 30 minutes ago. The placenta delivered intact. The midwife now reports a steady fresh trickle and an estimated blood loss of 1500 mL over the last 10 minutes. She has had 10 units IM oxytocin at delivery and bimanual uterine compression has been commenced. There is no perineal laceration above first degree.

On assessment she is alert and oriented but cool peripherally. Her fundus is at the umbilicus and boggy on palpation. She has no history of hypertension and no asthma. She has 2 large bore cannulae and a group and hold has been sent with crossmatch in progress. The anaesthetics registrar is on the way.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116/min, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert; cool peripheries; fundus at umbilicus, boggy; ongoing fresh PV bleeding; perineum first degree tear, sutured
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1500 mL; Hb on antenatal bloods 118 g/L (115-160); coags and lactate sent

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next uterotonic and adjunct?

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 mcg plus IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: She has no hypertension or pre-eclampsia, so ergometrine is the appropriate second-line uterotonic; TXA reduces death from bleeding when given within 3 hours of PPH onset (WOMAN trial). (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

options.text: IM carboprost 250 mcg repeated every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses
options.rationale: Carboprost (PGF2-alpha) is reserved when oxytocin and ergometrine have failed; jumping past ergometrine ignores the established uterotonic ladder and incurs bronchospasm risk. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

options.text: Misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum as the sole second-line agent
options.rationale: Misoprostol has slow onset and is an adjunct rather than first-line second agent; AU practice prefers ergometrine for rapid uterotonic effect. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

options.text: Insert Bakri balloon tamponade now without further pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is appropriate when pharmacological therapy fails or as a bridge to theatre; it should not replace a trial of second-line uterotonic in a haemodynamically stable woman. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

options.text: Continue uterine massage and further IV oxytocin infusion as sole therapy
options.rationale: Persisting with oxytocin alone after 1500 mL loss without additional uterotonic class change delays effective control; switching mechanism is the standard escalation. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

explanation.summary: Atonic postpartum haemorrhage with 1500 mL loss in a woman without hypertension or pre-eclampsia is escalated with ergometrine as the second-line uterotonic and intravenous tranexamic acid within 3 hours. The WOMAN trial established TXA mortality benefit in PPH. Carboprost and misoprostol are third-line agents for ongoing atony.
key_points: Uterotonic ladder: oxytocin → ergometrine (if no HT/PE) → carboprost (if no asthma) → misoprostol.
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset of PPH reduces mortality.
Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension, pre-eclampsia, and ischaemic heart disease.
Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma.
explanation.pearls: The single most missed step in PPH is intravenous TXA in the first 3 hours; it sits alongside the uterotonic ladder, not after it.

sources.label: RANZCOG Statement: Management of postpartum haemorrhage

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia BP control and magnesium
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar on call. A G1 primigravida at 32+4 weeks is referred from a regional hospital with severe pre-eclampsia. She reports an occipital headache and blurred vision for the past hour. She has no past medical history and was on no medications. The retrieval team is en route to transfer her to a tertiary centre with neonatal intensive care 90 minutes away.

On arrival in your obstetric unit she is alert and orientated but uncomfortable. Reflexes are brisk with no clonus. Foetal heart rate trace is reactive at 145/min with no decelerations. The midwife has just established a peripheral cannula and your consultant is on the way. Steroids for foetal lung maturity have been given by the referring hospital.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 168/115 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.8 °C, foetal HR 145/min
data_table.Examination: Alert, uncomfortable; reflexes brisk no clonus; epigastric tenderness; lower limb oedema 2+
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 95 x10^9/L (150-400), ALT 88 U/L (5-35), creatinine 92 micromol/L (45-90), urine PCR 800 mg/mmol, urate 0.42 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management bundle?

options.text: IV nifedipine 10 mg bolus plus IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose
options.rationale: Nifedipine is given orally, not intravenously, and the AU first-line IV antihypertensive in severe pre-eclampsia is labetalol or hydralazine; oral nifedipine is acceptable but not in IV form. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg plus IV magnesium 4 g loading then 1 g/hour, target BP <160/110
options.rationale: AU first-line acute antihypertensives in severe pre-eclampsia are IV labetalol or hydralazine, with magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour for seizure prophylaxis; BP target is under 160/110 mmHg. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: IV hydralazine 10 mg every 20 minutes plus dexamethasone 12 mg for foetal maturity
options.rationale: Steroids have already been given by the referring team and dose-repeating them is not indicated here; magnesium is the missing critical adjunct. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Continuous IV nicardipine infusion and immediate category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Immediate delivery without first achieving BP control and magnesium loading exposes the woman to intrapartum stroke and seizure; stabilisation precedes delivery decision. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Low-dose aspirin 150 mg orally plus magnesium 6 g IV loading dose only
options.rationale: Aspirin is for prevention of pre-eclampsia in at-risk women before 20 weeks; it has no role in acute management of established severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with headache, visual disturbance, and BP at or above 160/110 mmHg requires acute antihypertensive therapy plus magnesium seizure prophylaxis. AU first-line IV agents are labetalol and hydralazine; nifedipine is oral. Magnesium sulfate is given as a 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes then a 1 g/hour maintenance infusion, with monitoring of reflexes and respiratory rate for toxicity.
key_points: AU first-line acute IV antihypertensive: labetalol or hydralazine; nifedipine is oral.
BP target in severe pre-eclampsia under 160/110 mmHg.
Magnesium sulfate 4 g loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for seizure prophylaxis.
Calcium gluconate is the reversal antidote for magnesium toxicity.
explanation.pearls: Stabilise first, deliver second: severe pre-eclampsia needs BP control and magnesium before transfer or delivery, not in parallel.

subtopic_detail: Shoulder dystocia escalation after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure
stem:
You are the senior O and G registrar attending an emergency call from the labour ward. A G2P1 woman at 38+0 weeks has just delivered the foetal head spontaneously after a 2 hour second stage with epidural anaesthesia. The head is turtling against the perineum and the shoulders have not delivered with the next contraction. The estimated foetal weight on the 36 week growth scan was 4100 g.

The team has applied McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal hips and suprapubic pressure (Rubin I) for the past 30 seconds without delivery. The midwife is timing each manoeuvre and the obstetric consultant has been paged and is 4 minutes away. The mother is calm with epidural anaesthesia.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal pulse 95/min, BP 122/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, foetal HR last recorded 140/min before head delivery
data_table.Examination: Foetal head delivered, turtling sign present; shoulders not delivered; perineum intact apart from small first-degree graze; bladder catheterised
data_table.Investigations: Estimated foetal weight 4100 g at 36 weeks; no GDM; previous baby 3650 g; current head-to-body delivery interval 60 seconds

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Continue McRoberts and suprapubic pressure for another 60 seconds before escalating
options.rationale: Sustained reliance on first-line manoeuvres beyond 30 seconds without delivery delays effective release; each manoeuvre is allowed approximately 30 seconds before escalation. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Zavanelli (cephalic replacement) and prepare theatre for category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort manoeuvre when all other manoeuvres including internal rotations and posterior arm delivery have failed; it is associated with severe foetal injury when used too early. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Internal rotation manoeuvre (Rubin II or Wood's screw) or delivery of the posterior arm
options.rationale: After 30 seconds of failed McRoberts and suprapubic pressure, the HELPERR escalation moves to internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Wood's screw) or delivery of the posterior arm, both of which significantly improve resolution rates. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Symphysiotomy under local anaesthesia at the bedside before any further manoeuvre
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is reserved for resource-limited settings where caesarean is unavailable and carries high maternal morbidity; it is not part of the Australian shoulder dystocia algorithm. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Deliberate clavicle fracture by direct suprasternal pressure as next manoeuvre
options.rationale: Deliberate clavicle fracture is a last-resort manoeuvre after internal manoeuvres have failed; performing it before internal rotation skips effective lower-risk steps. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Shoulder dystocia)

explanation.summary: After approximately 30 seconds of unsuccessful McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure in shoulder dystocia, the HELPERR mnemonic escalates to internal manoeuvres (Rubin II or Wood's screw) or delivery of the posterior arm. Zavanelli, symphysiotomy, and deliberate clavicle fracture are last-resort manoeuvres reserved for failure of all other techniques.
key_points: HELPERR mnemonic: call for Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotations), Remove posterior arm, Roll (all fours).
Allow approximately 30 seconds per manoeuvre before escalating.
Internal manoeuvres and posterior arm delivery resolve most cases that fail first-line manoeuvres.
Document time of head delivery, each manoeuvre and its duration, and time of body delivery.
explanation.pearls: The commonest error in shoulder dystocia is staying too long with McRoberts; the next step at 30 seconds is internal rotation or posterior arm, not more pulling.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline No 42 Shoulder dystocia

subtopic_detail: Cord prolapse in regional setting with delayed transfer
stem:
You are the sole obstetric medical officer at a small regional hospital without on-site theatre or operative obstetric capability. A G2P1 woman at 37+5 weeks with a breech presentation presents with spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes ago and has just been transferred from triage. On vaginal examination you palpate a pulsating umbilical cord below the presenting part. Cervix is 4 cm dilated.

The nearest tertiary maternity unit with theatre is 90 minutes away by ambulance. The retrieval team is being mobilised. The midwife is supporting the woman in left lateral position. Foetal heart rate by intermittent auscultation is 130/min with no decelerations heard so far. The mother is calm and oriented, in mild discomfort. You have IV access established.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal pulse 92/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 130/min by Pinard
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, breech presentation, pulsating cord palpable below presenting part
data_table.Investigations: Foetal HR 130/min reassuring; bedside ultrasound confirms ongoing cardiac activity

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management before retrieval?

options.text: Vertical transfer with continuous digital elevation of the presenting part throughout
options.rationale: Sustained digital elevation for 90 minutes en route is impractical and unreliable; AU guidance favours retrograde bladder filling for delayed transfer to maintain elevation hands-free. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Manually replace the cord into the uterus and observe with continuous CTG monitoring
options.rationale: Cord replacement (funic reduction) is not recommended outside controlled trials due to risk of vasospasm and further prolapse; standard practice is elevation of the presenting part, not replacement of the cord. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Allow labour to proceed expectantly at 4 cm cervical dilatation given breech presentation
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with reassuring foetal heart rate still requires expedited delivery (usually caesarean if not imminently deliverable); awaiting full dilatation risks foetal demise. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Knee-chest, retrograde bladder fill 500-700 mL warm saline via Foley, urgent caesarean
options.rationale: When transfer is delayed, AU and UK practice is bladder filling with 500-700 mL of warmed saline via Foley to elevate the presenting part hands-free, combined with knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg positioning; urgent caesarean is the definitive treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Augment with IV oxytocin infusion to expedite vaginal delivery despite cord prolapse
options.rationale: Augmentation of labour worsens cord compression with each contraction; oxytocin is contraindicated in cord prolapse and delivery must be by caesarean unless vaginal birth is imminent. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Cord prolapse)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with delayed transfer is managed by retrograde bladder filling with warmed saline to elevate the presenting part hands-free, combined with knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg positioning during transport. This is the AU and UK approach when sustained digital elevation throughout a long ambulance journey is impractical. Definitive treatment is urgent caesarean section unless vaginal birth is imminent and the foetal heart rate remains reassuring.
key_points: Retrograde fill the bladder with 500-700 mL warm saline via Foley for hands-free elevation.
Position in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg during transfer.
Avoid manual cord replacement (funic reduction) and tocolysis is debated.
Definitive treatment is urgent caesarean unless vaginal birth is imminent.
explanation.pearls: For cord prolapse with a 90 minute transfer, fill the bladder and tip the woman head-down; sustained digital elevation in transit is not reliable.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline No 50 Cord prolapse

subtopic_detail: Maternal cardiac arrest perimortem caesarean timing
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in a tertiary ED resus when a 32-year-old G1 at 28+2 weeks gestation collapses suddenly at 11:02 with witnessed cardiac arrest. CPR is commenced immediately with continuous chest compressions, manual left uterine displacement, bag-mask ventilation, and intravenous adrenaline 1 mg every 3 to 4 minutes. By 11:05 she has had three cycles of ACLS without return of spontaneous circulation.

A brief bedside ultrasound earlier in the day showed a normal foetal heart rate of 145/min at 28+2 weeks. The fundus is palpable above the umbilicus. The ED consultant is performing the airway and the anaesthetist has arrived. A surgical pack is in the resus bay. The team confirms ongoing high-quality CPR and pulselessness.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal pulseless, no organised rhythm; ETCO2 18 mmHg during chest compressions; foetal HR not yet rechecked since arrest
data_table.Examination: Pulseless on continuous CPR; fundus 2 cm above umbilicus; no obvious bleeding; left uterine displacement maintained
data_table.Investigations: Earlier same-day ultrasound foetal HR 145/min; rhythm strip shows pulseless electrical activity

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next action?

options.text: Continue CPR for another 10 minutes before considering caesarean section
options.rationale: Delay beyond 4-5 minutes of arrest with a viable gravid uterus reduces both maternal resuscitation success and foetal neurological outcome; the 4 minute rule mandates earlier action. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10 Resuscitation in pregnancy)

options.text: Perform bedside ultrasound to confirm foetal viability before deciding on caesarean
options.rationale: Perimortem caesarean is performed primarily for maternal benefit (improving venous return); foetal viability is not a prerequisite at any gestation when uterus is above the umbilicus. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10 Resuscitation in pregnancy)

options.text: Transfer urgently to theatre for caesarean section under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Transfer interrupts CPR and adds delay; perimortem caesarean is performed at the site of arrest with minimal preparation, not in theatre. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10 Resuscitation in pregnancy)

options.text: Cease resuscitation given likely amniotic fluid embolism or massive pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: Ceasing resuscitation at 3 minutes of arrest without exhausting reversible causes is premature and forfeits the benefit of caesarean-assisted maternal CPR. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10 Resuscitation in pregnancy)

options.text: Perimortem caesarean in resus bay at 11:06 (within 4 minutes of arrest), continue CPR
options.rationale: ANZCOR and AU resuscitation in pregnancy guidance mandates perimortem caesarean within 4-5 minutes of maternal arrest when uterus is above the umbilicus, performed at the site of arrest to improve maternal venous return and oxygen delivery; foetal outcome is secondary. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10 Resuscitation in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Perimortem caesarean section is performed within 4 to 5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest when the uterus is palpable above the umbilicus, primarily to improve maternal venous return and oxygenation by relieving aortocaval compression. The procedure is done at the site of arrest, not in theatre, with CPR continuing throughout. Foetal viability is not a prerequisite to proceed.
key_points: 4-5 minute rule: incise at 4 minutes if no ROSC and uterus above umbilicus.
Performed at the site of arrest; do not transfer to theatre.
Primary indication is maternal benefit; foetus is a secondary beneficiary.
Continue CPR throughout and maintain left uterine displacement until uterus is emptied.
explanation.pearls: If the fundus is above the umbilicus and you do not have ROSC by 4 minutes, the scalpel goes in next; ultrasound for foetal heart is not a prerequisite.

subtopic_detail: Severe lithium toxicity with renal impairment
stem:
You are the medical registrar in a tertiary ED reviewing a 56-year-old woman with bipolar disorder on lithium 1000 mg daily for 20 years. She reports 3 days of diarrhoea and vomiting from a presumed gastroenteritis and now feels weak and confused. Her usual lithium level a month ago was 0.8 mmol/L with normal renal function.

On arrival she is drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 12. There is coarse tremor of both upper limbs, marked dysarthria, and broad-based ataxic gait when standing. She is on no other psychotropic medications and the family deny intentional overdose. Her recent regular bloods show baseline eGFR of 68 mL/min. The toxicology registrar has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5); coarse tremor; marked dysarthria; broad-based ataxic gait
data_table.Investigations: Serum lithium 3.6 mmol/L (0.4-1.0); Na 144 mmol/L (135-145), K 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); creatinine 168 micromol/L (45-90), eGFR 35 mL/min; ECG QT interval prolonged with T wave flattening

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.text: Immediate haemodialysis with planned 6-8 hour session and recheck level after rebound
options.rationale: EXTRIP and Australian poisons guidance recommend haemodialysis for serum lithium above 2.5 mmol/L with severe neurological impairment or renal impairment; haemodialysis efficiently clears lithium and must be followed by re-measurement due to redistribution rebound. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous 0.9% saline at 1 L per hour with repeat lithium level every 4 hours
options.rationale: Saline diuresis alone is inadequate at a level of 3.6 mmol/L with renal impairment and severe neurological features; relying on it delays definitive treatment and risks ongoing toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally plus intravenous 0.9% saline at 250 mL/hour
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind lithium and is ineffective for lithium toxicity; saline alone is insufficient at this severity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Cease lithium, observe in the high-dependency unit, recheck level in 24 hours
options.rationale: Ceasing the drug without active removal at a level of 3.6 mmol/L with neurological features and renal impairment carries a high risk of permanent neurotoxicity (SILENT syndrome). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol solution via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Whole bowel irrigation is reserved for sustained-release lithium ingestion (acute on chronic) with no neurological features; in this severe chronic case it has minimal yield and delays haemodialysis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Severe chronic lithium toxicity (level above 2.5 mmol/L with neurological impairment or AKI, or any level above 4 mmol/L) requires haemodialysis. Saline diuresis is inadequate at this severity. Lithium is not bound by activated charcoal and benefits from extracorporeal clearance, with a rebound level expected 6 hours after dialysis due to intracellular redistribution.
key_points: Haemodialysis criteria (EXTRIP): level above 4 mmol/L; or above 2.5 with severe symptoms or AKI; or rising despite saline.
Charcoal does not bind lithium; whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release acute on chronic only.
Expect rebound rise 6 hours after dialysis from intracellular redistribution.
SILENT (syndrome of irreversible lithium-effectuated neurotoxicity) is the long-term consequence of delayed treatment.
explanation.pearls: A chronic lithium level of 3.6 mmol/L with confusion and ataxia is a renal unit call, not just a saline drip; the rebound at 6 hours is the trap.

stem:
You are the on-call psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man on day 19 of clozapine titration for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. He is currently on 300 mg per day and has had a 3 day history of malaise, low-grade fever, and a new sense of mild shortness of breath. His last clozapine therapeutic drug monitoring was within range and the haematology monitoring shows an absolute neutrophil count of 4.2 x10^9/L.

On examination he describes mild left-sided chest discomfort that is worse on inspiration. He is mildly tachycardic at rest with no peripheral oedema. He has no risk factors for pulmonary embolism, no recent travel, no infective contacts, and no clinical signs of an upper respiratory tract infection. His mother (who attends with him) asks if his medication might be the cause.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 37.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild left-sided pleuritic chest discomfort; no peripheral oedema; chest clear; heart sounds dual no murmur
data_table.Investigations: ANC 4.2 x10^9/L (2.0-7.5); clozapine TDM within range two weeks ago; no recent troponin or echocardiogram

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue clozapine; prescribe paracetamol and arrange GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Clozapine-induced myocarditis peaks during weeks 2 to 6 of titration; missing it carries high mortality, and the picture of fever, tachycardia, and pleuritic chest pain mandates immediate cardiac workup. (Source: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine)
source_refs: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

options.text: Cease clozapine; perform troponin, ECG, CRP, BNP and transthoracic echocardiogram
options.rationale: The constellation of fever, tachycardia, and pleuritic chest pain during weeks 2 to 6 of clozapine titration is the textbook presentation of clozapine-induced myocarditis; AU practice is immediate cease, troponin and BNP, ECG, CRP, and echocardiogram. (Source: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine)
source_refs: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

options.text: Increase clozapine to the therapeutic 500 mg per day to settle his presentation
options.rationale: Increasing the dose during suspected myocarditis worsens the inflammation; the immediate step is cease and investigate, not titrate up. (Source: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine)
source_refs: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

options.text: Reduce clozapine to 100 mg per day and add valproate for sedation and titration
options.rationale: Continuing clozapine at any dose during suspected myocarditis is inappropriate; valproate adds metabolic burden without addressing the cardiac risk. (Source: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine)
source_refs: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

options.text: Cease clozapine and switch to olanzapine the same day as a substitute
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotic during acute clozapine myocarditis workup risks confounding the picture and adds anticholinergic and metabolic load; stabilise the cardiac issue before reintroducing antipsychotic. (Source: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine)
source_refs: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis peaks during weeks 2 to 6 of titration and presents with fever, tachycardia, malaise, and often pleuritic chest pain. The Australian recommended response is immediate cessation, troponin, BNP, CRP, ECG, and echocardiogram. The neutrophil count is not the key marker for myocarditis; that monitoring system addresses agranulocytosis, not cardiac inflammation.
key_points: Clozapine myocarditis risk peaks weeks 2 to 6 of titration.
Cease clozapine on suspicion; troponin, BNP, CRP, ECG, echocardiogram.
ANC monitoring is for agranulocytosis, separate from myocarditis.
Rechallenge after myocarditis is specialist-led and rarely undertaken.
explanation.pearls: Fever plus tachycardia in week 3 of clozapine is myocarditis until disproven by troponin and BNP; do not blame a viral illness without ruling it out.

sources.label: RANZCP CALC Clinical Practice Guideline for Clozapine

stem:
You are the medical registrar called to assess a 45-year-old man on the acute psychiatry ward. He has been on haloperidol 5 mg twice daily for 1 month for schizophrenia. Over the past 3 days he has developed fever, generalised rigidity, fluctuating consciousness, and autonomic instability. He has had no recent change in medication and no other psychotropic exposure.

On examination he is drowsy with lead-pipe rigidity in all four limbs. He is diaphoretic and his skin feels clammy. His Glasgow Coma Scale fluctuates between 12 and 14. He has been incontinent of urine. He is on no medications other than haloperidol. The intensive care registrar has been called and the bed manager is finding a bed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132/min, BP 180/110 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 39.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 12-14; lead-pipe rigidity all limbs; diaphoretic; urinary incontinence; pupils equal and reactive
data_table.Investigations: CK 4500 U/L (40-200); WCC 16 x10^9/L (4-11); K 3.6 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); creatinine 142 micromol/L (45-90); ABG metabolic acidosis pH 7.28

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management bundle?

options.text: Continue haloperidol; active cooling with paracetamol 1 g rectally only
options.rationale: Continuing the precipitating antipsychotic in NMS perpetuates the syndrome and risks fatal hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, and arrhythmia; cessation is the foundational step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 5 mg as first-line therapy plus paracetamol for fever
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are an adjunct but not a definitive treatment for NMS; the priority is to cease the antipsychotic, cool, hydrate, and add specific therapy (dantrolene or bromocriptine) for severe cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Cease haloperidol, commence IV fluids and cooling, give IV dantrolene 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: Severe NMS with rigidity, hyperthermia above 38.5 °C, and rhabdomyolysis is managed by ceasing the antipsychotic, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, dantrolene for muscle rigidity, and ICU admission; bromocriptine is an alternative dopaminergic agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch to clozapine immediately and continue current psychiatric care plan
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotic during active NMS is contraindicated; reintroduction of any antipsychotic is delayed until full resolution and is specialist-led. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Intravenous cyproheptadine 12 mg empirically for presumed serotonin syndrome
options.rationale: Cyproheptadine is a serotonin antagonist for serotonin syndrome and has no role in NMS; the picture of haloperidol exposure and lead-pipe rigidity points to NMS, not serotonin syndrome. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is managed by ceasing the offending antipsychotic, aggressive supportive care (IV fluids, cooling), and specific pharmacotherapy with dantrolene for muscle rigidity or bromocriptine as a dopaminergic agent. Severity is graded by temperature, CK, and autonomic instability. ICU admission is appropriate for severe presentations.
key_points: NMS specific agents: dantrolene 1-2.5 mg/kg IV (rigidity) or bromocriptine 2.5 mg orally TDS (dopamine agonist).
Cyproheptadine is for serotonin syndrome, not NMS; the two differ in muscle tone, reflexes, and trigger drug.
Aggressive cooling and IV fluids to limit rhabdomyolysis and renal failure.
Reintroduction of any antipsychotic is delayed at least 2 weeks after resolution and specialist-led.
explanation.pearls: Lead-pipe rigidity with haloperidol exposure is NMS (dantrolene), not serotonin syndrome (cyproheptadine); reflexes are reduced or absent in NMS but brisk in serotonin syndrome.

stem:
You are the consultation psychiatry registrar seeing a 28-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder. She has been stable for 2 years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, with no relapse since starting the medication after one severe manic episode requiring inpatient admission. She and her partner attend together to discuss pregnancy planning. She is on the combined oral contraceptive pill currently, with regular cycles when not on the pill.

Her past history includes one episode of mania requiring inpatient admission 4 years ago and one episode of severe postnatal depression after a previous pregnancy with another partner. She is not currently depressed. She has no other medical issues and is otherwise well. Her GP has provided pre-pregnancy folate counselling.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic; cognition intact; insight preserved; no current psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: Last valproate level therapeutic; FBC, LFT, UEC within normal limits; pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose with high-dose folate 5 mg daily peri-conceptually
options.rationale: Folate does not abolish the dose-dependent neurodevelopmental and neural tube defect risk of valproate; valproate carries the highest teratogenic burden of mood stabilisers and AU practice avoids it in women planning pregnancy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic, perinatal section)

options.text: Switch valproate to carbamazepine at therapeutic dose before conception
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also teratogenic (neural tube defects, cardiac, urinary) and not preferred over lamotrigine or lithium in bipolar pregnancy planning. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch to lithium 600 mg daily before conception, accepting Ebstein anomaly risk
options.rationale: Lithium is a reasonable option in pregnancy for bipolar I prophylaxis (especially if previously responsive) but lamotrigine has a more favourable teratogenic profile; lithium is not the universally preferred next agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Cross-titrate to lamotrigine and cease valproate before conception
options.rationale: Lamotrigine has the most favourable perinatal safety profile among mood stabilisers and is preferred for cross-titration before conception in stable bipolar disorder; valproate is avoided in women of reproductive age due to teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental impact. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Cease all mood stabilisers and monitor monthly with mental state examination only
options.rationale: Ceasing prophylaxis in bipolar I with previous severe mania and postnatal depression carries an unacceptably high relapse risk; a safer pregnancy-compatible agent should be substituted. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Valproate is the most teratogenic mood stabiliser and is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy or of reproductive potential unless other options are ineffective. Lamotrigine has the most favourable perinatal profile and is the preferred alternative for a stable patient with bipolar I disorder. Lithium remains an option in selected cases but carries the small risk of Ebstein anomaly and requires close level monitoring across pregnancy.
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity is dose-dependent and not abolished by folate.
Lamotrigine is preferred for cross-titration before conception when patient is stable.
Lithium remains an option if lamotrigine inadequate; level monitoring intensifies in pregnancy.
Pre-conception planning includes contraception continuation until cross-titration complete.
explanation.pearls: Stable bipolar I on valproate planning pregnancy: cross-titrate to lamotrigine before stopping contraception, not after conception.

stem:
You are the ED registrar reviewing a 24-year-old woman who has presented 2 hours after taking 30 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (estimated 750 mg) in an impulsive act after an argument. She was found by her flatmate and brought straight in by ambulance. She is normally on no medications and has no medical comorbidities. There is no other substance involved on history.

On arrival she is drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 11. Her airway is patent. She has had no seizures. The toxicology consultant has been contacted and you have access to a HDU bed if needed. The ECG strip is showing a widened QRS complex and a prominent terminal R wave in lead aVR. Her potassium is on the low side of normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 11 (E2 V4 M5); airway patent; pupils dilated 6 mm sluggish to light; bowel sounds reduced
data_table.Investigations: ECG QRS 140 ms (under 120 normal), terminal R wave aVR 4 mm; K 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); Na 138 mmol/L; venous gas pH 7.32, lactate 2.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous midazolam 2 mg as sole therapy for sedation while observing
options.rationale: Sedation does not reverse TCA cardiotoxicity; the priority is sodium bicarbonate for QRS prolongation and seizure prophylaxis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg to reverse drowsiness while observing for seizure
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose or unknown overdose due to seizure risk, and does not reverse TCA toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube only after airway protection
options.rationale: Charcoal is of limited value beyond 1 hour post-ingestion and does not reverse established cardiotoxicity; sodium bicarbonate addresses the immediate life-threatening QRS prolongation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Whole bowel irrigation has no role in TCA overdose; sodium bicarbonate is the cardiotoxicity-specific intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, target QRS <120 ms and pH 7.5
options.rationale: TCA cardiotoxicity (QRS above 100 ms, terminal R in aVR above 3 mm, hypotension) is treated with IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, titrated to QRS narrowing and pH 7.45 to 7.55; the alkalinisation increases protein binding and the sodium load overcomes the sodium channel block. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA cardiotoxicity is identified by QRS prolongation above 100 ms, terminal R wave in aVR above 3 mm, and hypotension. The specific intervention is intravenous sodium bicarbonate boluses to alkalinise and overcome sodium channel block. Seizures, hypotension, and ventricular arrhythmias are the principal causes of death.
key_points: Sodium bicarbonate dose: 1-2 mmol/kg boluses, repeat to QRS under 120 ms and arterial pH 7.45-7.55.
ECG markers of toxicity: QRS above 100 ms, R wave aVR above 3 mm, R:S ratio aVR above 0.7.
Hypokalaemia worsens arrhythmia risk; correct to normal but expect intracellular shifts with bicarbonate.
Lipid emulsion considered in refractory cardiotoxicity after toxicology advice.
explanation.pearls: QRS above 100 ms with a terminal R wave in aVR after TCA overdose is the cue for sodium bicarbonate, not benzodiazepines.

subtopic_detail: ECT for refusing involuntary patient with capacity
stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 67-year-old woman currently admitted involuntarily under the Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 with severe major depression with psychotic features. Over the past 3 weeks she has refused all oral medications, lost 8 kg, become bed-bound, and is now requiring nasogastric feeding for nutritional support. She voices the wish to die imminently and refuses fluids when not on the NG.

Your consultant psychiatrist has assessed her as competent to make decisions about her treatment but as having severe melancholic depression life-threatening in its trajectory. The family supports treatment with electroconvulsive therapy. She refuses ECT when offered, stating that she does not deserve to be treated. The treating team agrees ECT is life-saving and the only realistic short-term option for response.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 58/min, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Melancholic appearance; psychomotor retardation; nihilistic delusion of decay; reduced reactivity to environment
data_table.Investigations: Albumin 28 g/L (35-50); K 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); ECG normal sinus; ferritin 18 micrograms/L (30-300)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step to enable treatment?

options.text: Apply to the Mental Health Tribunal for an order authorising ECT under the Act
options.rationale: Under Australian state mental health legislation (including Victorian MHWA 2022 and NSW MHA 2007), ECT for an involuntary patient who refuses requires authorisation by the Mental Health Tribunal regardless of the patient's capacity status; emergency provisions exist for life-threatening situations but the Tribunal pathway is the primary route. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria; RANZCP Position Statement on ECT)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

options.text: Two senior doctors co-sign emergency ECT consent without further authorisation
options.rationale: Two-doctor authorisation alone is not the standard pathway for non-emergent ECT for a refusing involuntary patient; tribunal authorisation is required for planned ECT in this group. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

options.text: Treat under common-law necessity without further legal authorisation
options.rationale: Common-law necessity is not the proper route when the Mental Health Act provides a defined statutory pathway for ECT in involuntary patients. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

options.text: Obtain consent from the substitute decision-maker (next-of-kin) and proceed
options.rationale: Substitute consent does not override the statutory consent requirements for ECT under state Mental Health Acts when the patient is involuntary and refusing; tribunal authorisation is required. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

options.text: Discharge after capacity assessment given she retains decisional capacity
options.rationale: She is involuntary, severely depressed, refusing fluids, and at acute risk of death; discharge in this setting breaches duty of care and the criteria for involuntary treatment remain met. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

explanation.summary: ECT for a refusing involuntary patient under Australian state Mental Health legislation requires authorisation by the relevant Mental Health Tribunal, not just senior doctor sign-off, substitute consent, or common-law necessity. Capacity does not bypass this process when the patient is detained as an involuntary patient. Emergency provisions exist for true life-threatening urgency but the standard pathway is the Tribunal.
key_points: Mental Health Tribunal authorisation is required for ECT in refusing involuntary patients across Australian jurisdictions.
Substitute decision-maker consent does not override the Mental Health Act ECT process.
Emergency ECT provisions exist (varying by state) but require strict criteria.
Capacity assessment is documented but does not change the statutory ECT pathway for involuntary patients.
explanation.pearls: An involuntary patient refusing ECT goes to the Mental Health Tribunal, not to two doctors with a consent form; states differ but the principle is uniform.

sources.label: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 Victoria

stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 23-year-old woman in the medical ward after a new-onset psychiatric presentation 5 days ago. She is mute, immobile, and has been observed maintaining unusual limb postures for over an hour. Examination reveals waxy flexibility and intermittent grimacing. She has not eaten or drunk in 48 hours and has had no exposure to antipsychotic medication. She presented initially with disorganised speech and anxiety which then progressed to her current state.

This morning her temperature has risen to 38.4 °C and her creatine kinase is elevated. Neurology and infectious diseases have been consulted. Brain MRI yesterday was unremarkable. CSF cell count, glucose, and protein were normal but anti-NMDA receptor antibodies have been sent and are pending. The team is asking what to do next while waiting for those results.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116/min, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 38.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile; waxy flexibility; sustained limb posturing; intermittent grimacing; reduced oral intake 48 hours
data_table.Investigations: CK 1800 U/L (40-200); FBC and electrolytes within normal limits; brain MRI unremarkable; CSF cell count normal; anti-NMDA receptor antibody result pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate diagnostic and therapeutic step?

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg as antipsychotic challenge to test for psychosis
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are not the diagnostic or therapeutic tool of choice for suspected catatonia. (Source: RANZCP Practice Resources: Catatonia)

options.text: IV lorazepam 2 mg as diagnostic and therapeutic challenge, parallel ECT planning
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV) is both diagnostic (rapid partial response within minutes to hours) and therapeutic for catatonia, regardless of underlying cause; parallel ECT planning is appropriate as ECT is the definitive treatment for refractory catatonia. (Source: RANZCP Practice Resources: Catatonia)

options.text: Intravenous propofol 1 mg/kg for sedation and disimpaction of posturing
options.rationale: Propofol is not used for catatonia treatment; lorazepam challenge is the AU recommended first-line diagnostic-therapeutic approach. (Source: RANZCP Practice Resources: Catatonia)

options.text: Oral bromocriptine 7.5 mg twice daily for presumed neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: There is no neuroleptic exposure, so NMS is unlikely; bromocriptine is for NMS-specific management and is not first-line for catatonia of any cause. (Source: RANZCP Practice Resources: Catatonia)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g empirically while awaiting anti-NMDA receptor antibody
options.rationale: CSF is bland and there is no meningoencephalitis evidence; empirical antibacterial cover is not justified and does not address the catatonia itself. (Source: RANZCP Practice Resources: Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is treated with a lorazepam challenge as both diagnostic and therapeutic step, regardless of the underlying cause (psychiatric, medical, or autoimmune). A partial response within minutes to hours confirms the diagnosis; ongoing benzodiazepine and parallel ECT planning are appropriate as ECT is the definitive treatment for refractory or malignant catatonia. Antipsychotics can precipitate NMS and must be avoided.
key_points: Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is the AU first-line challenge for catatonia.
Avoid antipsychotics during active catatonia (NMS risk).
ECT is the definitive treatment if lorazepam fails or in malignant catatonia.
Investigate underlying cause in parallel (anti-NMDA, medical, structural).
explanation.pearls: Posturing plus waxy flexibility plus fever and elevated CK without neuroleptic exposure is malignant catatonia; the answer is lorazepam now and ECT next, not haloperidol.

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome after MAOI plus tramadol
stem:
You are the ED registrar called to assess a 38-year-old woman with a 4 year history of refractory depression on phenelzine 60 mg daily who has just been brought in. Her GP prescribed tramadol 100 mg for a severe migraine 4 hours ago, unaware of the MAOI. She took 1 tablet and has rapidly developed agitation, sweating, and shivering. Her husband reports she has been increasingly hot and jittery and she has been vomiting twice.

On examination she is restless and confused with marked diaphoresis. She has rhythmic horizontal eye movements (ocular clonus), hyperreflexia particularly in the lower limbs with sustained ankle clonus, and increased lower limb tone. Pupils are dilated. She is on no other psychotropics and has no infective contacts. She has had no antipsychotic exposure. Phenelzine has been ceased today.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 162/98 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 39.1 °C
data_table.Examination: Restless, confused; ocular clonus; lower limb hyperreflexia with sustained ankle clonus; pupils dilated; bowel sounds increased; lower limb hypertonia greater than upper limb
data_table.Investigations: CK 1200 U/L (40-200); K 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); creatinine 96 micromol/L (45-90); venous gas pH 7.36, lactate 2.8 mmol/L; FBC and coags pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg plus surface cooling with cold packs only
options.rationale: Haloperidol increases the risk of NMS and worsens hyperthermia in serotonin syndrome; antipsychotics should be avoided in the acute setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous dantrolene 1 mg/kg as first-line agent for hyperthermia
options.rationale: Dantrolene is used in NMS or malignant hyperthermia, not as first-line in serotonin syndrome; benzodiazepines and supportive care are the AU first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Cease both agents, give IV benzodiazepines plus cooling plus IV fluids
options.rationale: AU management of serotonin syndrome is cessation of the offending agents, IV benzodiazepines as first-line for agitation and hyperthermia, aggressive cooling and IV fluids; cyproheptadine (PO only, no IV preparation in Australia) is reserved as adjunct in moderate-severe non-responders. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous cyproheptadine 32 mg loading dose immediately as primary therapy
options.rationale: There is no parenteral cyproheptadine available in Australia; oral or NG cyproheptadine is the only route, and benzodiazepines come first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg for presumed TCA-like cardiotoxicity
options.rationale: Tramadol and phenelzine together cause serotonin syndrome, not sodium channel block; sodium bicarbonate has no role here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome from a MAOI plus tramadol is managed by ceasing offending agents, supportive care with IV benzodiazepines, aggressive cooling and IV fluids, with cyproheptadine reserved as an oral adjunct in moderate to severe non-responders. Antipsychotics are avoided as they may worsen hyperthermia and confuse the clinical picture with NMS. The discriminator from NMS is hyperreflexia (especially lower limbs) and clonus, versus rigidity and reduced reflexes in NMS.
key_points: Distinguishing features: serotonin syndrome causes hyperreflexia and clonus (lower limb predominant); NMS causes lead-pipe rigidity and reduced reflexes.
Cyproheptadine is oral only in Australia; no IV preparation.
Antipsychotics worsen serotonin syndrome and risk diagnostic confusion with NMS.
MAOIs plus serotonergic agents (SSRI, SNRI, tramadol, pethidine, linezolid) are the highest-risk combinations.
explanation.pearls: Lower limb clonus with diaphoresis after a MAOI plus tramadol is serotonin syndrome; the answer is benzodiazepines and cooling, with oral cyproheptadine as a backup.
